hso_hl.objective: Records of processing activities are established through **parallel documentation regimes** that produce records of processing activities, technical documentation, certification records, information security policy documentation, and quality management system documentation serving distinct audit purposes.
considerations: **5 of 5 regulations participate** on **parallel artefacts** with distinct triggers, retention cadences, and recipients — GDPR Art. 30(1)/(2)/(3)/(4)/(5) RoPA (DPA audit); NIS 2 Art. 24(1)/(2)/(3) certification-driven records per Member State (Member State / competent authority audit); CRA Annex VII §1-§8 8-item technical documentation + Art. 13(12)/(13)/(15)-(25)/(28)/(30)/(31)/(32) (MSA audit, 10-year or support-period retention); DORA Art. 9(4)(a) information security policy (NCA audit — **different record type from RoPA, DORA is fully absent for RoPA-equivalent per CRDA explicit note**); AI Act Art. 11 + Art. 12(1)/(2) + Art. 17(1) 13-element QMS + Art. 19(1) provider logs (MSA + AI Office audit).

**CRDA-deep verified (10 pairs: 2 SAME — DIFFERENT-PERSPECTIVE — GDPR ↔ NIS 2 + GDPR ↔ CRA — substantively scope-disjoint per CRDA `N (typically)`, different record types; + 3 SAME — COMPLEMENTARY classified by CRDA — GDPR ↔ AI Act, NIS 2 ↔ CRA, CRA ↔ AI Act; + 1 SAME — DIFFERENT-PERSPECTIVE RECOMMENDED-by-CRDA — GDPR ↔ DORA substantively scope-disjoint; + 4 SAME — COMPLEMENTARY RECOMMENDED-by-CRDA — NIS 2 ↔ DORA, NIS 2 ↔ AI Act, CRA ↔ DORA, DORA ↔ AI Act)** — no CORRECTED, no CONTRADICTORY.

**DORA-absence flag (CRDA-deep §D-09.4 line 29)**: DORA is fully absent for RoPA-equivalent — no analogue to GDPR Art. 30. For DORA financial entities, the GDPR RoPA remains the dedicated authority for processing-activities documentation; DORA Art. 9(4)(a) IS-policy documentation is a separate artefact.

**No OJ-level cross-regulation consolidation rule for documentation** — unlike D-09.2 where CRA Art. 13(4) sentence 2 permits AI Act Art. 9 risk-management to substitute for CRA risk assessment, that rule covers the *risk-assessment object* not the *documentation object*. The 5 documentation regimes operate in parallel; no OJ-permitted cross-substitution exists.

**Scope caveats**: (a) 3 substantively scope-disjoint pairs (GDPR ↔ NIS 2 + GDPR ↔ CRA + GDPR ↔ DORA) — different record types, different audit purposes; the high-level SO does NOT engage on these pairs alone; (b) the only cross-regulation link involving CRA + NIS 2 is the CRA Art. 27(8)/(9) CSA-certification presumption-of-conformity as upstream gate for NSIE procurements; (c) the 5 documentation regimes must be maintained in parallel even when the product is CRA-conformant and the AI system is AI-Act high-risk.
hso_hl.activation: auto  # activates when ≥2 applicable regs have ≥1 pair with Y or Conditional-Y-on-same-party scope overlap AND ≥1 sub-SO activates; for D-09.4 the matrix above shows **3 same-row pairs with N (typically)** (GDPR↔NIS 2, GDPR↔CRA, GDPR↔DORA-recommended — substantively scope-disjoint per CRDA) and **7 pairs with Y or Conditional-Y activation on the joint-condition operator** (GDPR↔AI_Act = Conditional-Y; NIS2↔CRA = Conditional-Y; NIS2↔DORA = Conditional-Y; NIS2↔AI_Act = Conditional-Y; CRA↔DORA = Conditional-Y; CRA↔AI_Act = Y; DORA↔AI_Act = Conditional-Y). The high-level SO engages on the 7 same-event activation pairs — not on the 3 substantively scope-disjoint pairs. The high-level SO does NOT collapse the 5 documentation regimes into a single artefact; it surfaces the **common documentation-regime duty** (records of processing activities + technical documentation + certification records + IS-policy documentation + QMS documentation) on parallel artefacts serving distinct audit purposes.

hso_per_reg.source_SR: SR-GDPR-048 + SR-GDPR-050 + SR-GDPR-065 + SR-GDPR-066 + SR-GDPR-067 + SR-GDPR-068
hso_per_reg.objective:
Each controller and processor **maintains a record of processing activities under its responsibility** (Art. 30(1) GDPR), in writing or electronic form (Art. 30(3) GDPR), containing the Art. 30(1) 7-item content list; the processor maintains a parallel record under Art. 30(2) GDPR; records are made available to the supervisory authority on request (Art. 30(4) GDPR), subject to the Art. 30(5) 250-employee exception.

**CSF anchors (from `SR-GDPR-048` + `SR-GDPR-050` + `SR-GDPR-065-068` v0.3 security_rationale):**
ID.AM-08 (primary), PR.DS-12, GV.PO-02.
*(ID.AM-08 — Systems, hardware, software, services, and data are managed throughout their life cycle — anchors the GDPR Art. 30 RoPA `record of processing activities under its responsibility` mapped to NIST CSF 2.0 ID.AM-08 per CRDA DomainAnalysis §D-09.4 line 113 cross-validation; PR.DS-12 — Data managed consistent with risk strategy — anchors the GDPR Art. 30(1)(g) `general description of the technical and organisational security measures` per CRDA DomainAnalysis §D-09.4 line 113; GV.PO-02 — Cybersecurity processes and procedures established, communicated, enforced — anchors the GDPR Art. 30(3) `in writing, including in electronic form` per CRDA DomainAnalysis §D-09.4 line 113 cross-validation (GDPR specifically maps GV.PO-02 for the demonstrable-compliance dimension). The three subcategories together enable the controller or processor to demonstrate ex post, against the Art. 5(2) accountability burden, that the Art. 30(1) RoPA 7-item content list + Art. 30(3) in-writing-or-electronic-form + Art. 30(4) on-DPA-request records were established, reviewed, and updated as processing activities changed (Art. 30(1) `shall maintain`). **IMPORTANT — DORA-absence flag**: per CRDA-deep §D-09.4 line 29, **DORA is fully absent for RoPA-equivalent**; for DORA financial entities, the GDPR RoPA remains the dedicated authority for processing-activities documentation. The GDPR RoPA does **NOT** substitute for the CRA Annex VII 8-item technical documentation (substantively scope-disjoint per CRDA `N (typically)`), the NIS 2 Art. 24 certification-driven records (substantively scope-disjoint per CRDA `N (typically)`), the DORA Art. 9(4)(a) IS-policy documentation (different record type), or the AI Act Annex IV + Art. 17(1) QMS documentation (parallel artefacts). Phase 1 entities with GDPR + CRA applicability must maintain both GDPR RoPA + CRA Annex VII technical documentation on distinct artefacts that are not substitutable.)*

Member States shall **encourage the use of European or internationally accepted standards and specifications relevant to the security of network and information systems** (Art. 24(1) sentence 1 NIS 2) and of qualified trust services (Art. 24(1) sentence 2); the Commission may adopt delegated acts specifying entities that must use certified ICT products, services and processes (Art. 24(2) NIS 2), and ENISA may be requested to prepare a candidate European cybersecurity certification scheme (Art. 24(3) NIS 2).

**CSF anchors (from `SR-NIS2-043` v0.3 security_rationale):**
ID.AM-08 (primary), PR.DS-12.
*(ID.AM-08 — Systems, hardware, software, services, and data are managed throughout their life cycle — anchors the NIS 2 Art. 24(1)/(2)/(3) certification-driven recordkeeping mapped to NIST CSF 2.0 ID.AM-08 per CRDA DomainAnalysis §D-09.4 line 113 cross-validation; PR.DS-12 — Data managed consistent with risk strategy — anchors the NIS 2 Art. 24(1) use of certified ICT products/services/processes per CRDA DomainAnalysis §D-09.4 line 113. The two subcategories together enable the NSIE to demonstrate ex post, against the Member State competent authority, that the Art. 24(1)/(2)/(3) certification-driven records were established for the certified ICT products/services/processes procured. **IMPORTANT — NIS 2 partial coverage at D-09.4**: the NIS 2 Art. 24(1)/(2)/(3) certification-driven recordkeeping is **partial coverage** — Member-State-driven use of certified ICT products/services/processes, not a RoPA-equivalent; substantively scope-disjoint from GDPR per CRDA `N (typically)` (different record types, different triggers). Phase 1 entities with NIS 2 + GDPR applicability must maintain both GDPR Art. 30 RoPA + NIS 2 Art. 24 certification-driven records on distinct artefacts that are not substitutable. For NSIEs that are CRA-conformant product procurers, the CRA Art. 27(8)/(9) CSA-certification presumption-of-conformity machinery is the upstream gate for NIS 2 procurements — Phase 1 must verify that procured CRA products hold CSA certification at the assurance level required by the Commission's delegated act under Art. 24(2). The **NIS 2 → CRA certification link** is the only cross-regulation link at D-09.4 involving NIS 2.)*

hso_per_reg.inherits_from: SO-CRA-055 + SO-CRA-056 + SO-CRA-057 + SO-CRA-058 + SO-CRA-059 + SO-CRA-061 + SO-CRA-062 + SO-CRA-063 + SO-CRA-064 + SO-CRA-065 + SO-CRA-066 + SO-CRA-067
hso_per_reg.objective:
The manufacturer **draws up before placing a product with digital elements on the market the technical documentation referred to in Art. 31** (Art. 13(12) sentence 1 CRA), carries out the conformity assessment procedure referred to in Art. 32, draws up the EU declaration of conformity under Art. 28, and affixes the CE marking under Art. 30 (Art. 13(12) sentence 2); the manufacturer keeps the technical documentation and the EU declaration of conformity at the disposal of market surveillance authorities for at least 10 years or the support period, whichever is longer (Art. 13(13) CRA).

**CSF anchors (from `SR-CRA-085` + `SR-CRA-087` + `SR-CRA-088` + `SR-CRA-078` v0.3 security_rationale):**
ID.AM-08 (primary), PR.DS-12, ID.RA-05.
*(ID.AM-08 — Systems, hardware, software, services, and data are managed throughout their life cycle — anchors the CRA Annex VII §1-§8 8-item content list mapped to NIST CSF 2.0 ID.AM-08 per CRDA DomainAnalysis §D-09.4 line 113 cross-validation; PR.DS-12 — Data managed consistent with risk strategy — anchors the CRA Annex VII §1 `general description` + §3 `cybersecurity risk assessment` dimensions per CRDA DomainAnalysis §D-09.4 line 113; ID.RA-05 — Threats, vulnerabilities, likelihoods, and impacts are used to determine the priority risk responses — anchors the CRA Annex VII §3 `cybersecurity risk assessment` documentation per CRDA DomainAnalysis §D-09.4 line 113 (CRA specifically maps ID.RA-05 for the risk-assessment documentation). The three subcategories together enable the manufacturer to demonstrate ex post, against the market surveillance authority, that the Annex VII §1-§8 8-item content list + Art. 13(12)/(13)/(15)/(16)/(17)/(19)/(20)/(21)/(22)/(23) documentation were established before placing on the market and kept up to date during the support period with the 10-year retention per Art. 13(13). **IMPORTANT — CRA full content codification**: CRA Annex VII §1-§8 is **fully content-codified** — the 8-item content list is explicit. The CRA sub-SO is the densest documentation regime of the 5 (covering product identification + manufacturer contact + SPOC + support-period + EU declaration + CE marking + conformity assessment + MSA cooperation + cessation-of-operations + Commission implementing/delegated acts). **IMPORTANT — CRA Annex VII does NOT cross-substitute for GDPR RoPA**: substantively scope-disjoint per CRDA `N (typically)` (different record types, different triggers, different audit purposes). **IMPORTANT — CRA + AI Act for AI-enabled products do NOT have OJ-level consolidation for documentation**: unlike the CRA Art. 13(4) sentence 2 risk-assessment consolidation rule at D-09.2 (which substitutes AI Act Art. 9 risk-management for CRA risk-assessment), there is no equivalent documentation-level consolidation rule at D-09.4; the two regimes maintain parallel artefacts (CRA Annex VII + AI Act Annex IV + AI Act Art. 17 QMS). **CRA Art. 27(8)/(9) CSA-certification presumption-of-conformity**: the CRA Art. 27(8)/(9) `A product with digital elements and the processes put in place by the manufacturer for which the manufacturer has obtained an EU statement of conformity or an EU certificate under a European cybersecurity certification scheme adopted pursuant to Regulation (EU) 2019/881, at assurance level at least 'substantial', shall be presumed to be in conformity with the essential cybersecurity requirements` is the upstream gate for NIS 2 procurements of CRA-conformant products; Phase 1 entities with NIS 2 + CRA applicability that procure CSA-certified CRA products discharge the Conditional-Y scope overlap at the certification boundary.)*

hso_per_reg.objective:
Financial entities **develop and document an information security policy defining rules to protect the availability, authenticity, integrity and confidentiality of data, information assets and ICT assets, including those of their customers, where applicable** (Art. 9(4)(a) DORA); this is entity-level IS-policy documentation serving NCA audit purposes and is a **different record type from the GDPR RoPA**.

**CSF anchors (from `SR-DORA-031` v0.3 security_rationale):**
ID.AM-08 (primary), PR.DS-12, GV.PO-01.
*(ID.AM-08 — Systems, hardware, software, services, and data are managed throughout their life cycle — anchors the DORA Art. 9(4)(a) `data, information assets and ICT assets` dimension mapped to NIST CSF 2.0 ID.AM-08 per CRDA DomainAnalysis §D-09.4 line 113 cross-validation; PR.DS-12 — Data managed consistent with risk strategy — anchors the DORA Art. 9(4)(a) `availability, authenticity, integrity and confidentiality of data, information assets and ICT assets, including those of their customers, where applicable` CIA+A 4-way + customer-where-applicable dimension per CRDA DomainAnalysis §D-09.4 line 113; GV.PO-01 — Organizational cybersecurity policy established, communicated, enforced — anchors the DORA Art. 9(4)(a) IS-policy as an organisational policy (cross to D-09.1 DORA sub-SO). The three subcategories together enable the financial entity to demonstrate ex post, against the national competent authority + EBA supervisory baseline, that the Art. 9(4)(a) IS-policy documentation was developed and documented in light of the proportionality factors (size, nature, scale, complexity per Art. 4). **IMPORTANT — DORA-absence flag**: per CRDA-deep §D-09.4 line 29, **DORA is fully absent for RoPA-equivalent** — DORA does not impose a RoPA obligation (no analogue to GDPR Art. 30). The DORA Art. 9(4)(a) IS-policy documentation is a **different record type** (entity-level IS-policy documentation) serving a **different audit purpose** (NCA audit on IS policy) than the GDPR RoPA (DPA audit on processing activities). For DORA financial entities, the GDPR RoPA remains the dedicated authority for processing-activities documentation; the DORA Art. 9(4)(a) IS-policy documentation is a separate artefact. **DORA BC/DR logging obligations (Art. 12) are operational continuity artefacts, not RoPA-equivalents** — covered separately under D-10 Monitoring & Audit. Phase 1 entities with GDPR + DORA applicability must maintain both GDPR RoPA + DORA Art. 9(4)(a) IS-policy documentation on distinct artefacts that are not substitutable.)*

Providers of high-risk AI systems **draw up and keep up-to-date the technical documentation of the high-risk AI system in accordance with Annex IV before that system is placed on the market or put into service** (Art. 11(1) AI Act), **keep the logs automatically recorded by the high-risk AI system to the extent under their control** (Art. 19(1) AI Act), and document the **quality management system** in written policies, procedures, and instructions covering at least the 13 enumerated aspects (a)-(m) (Art. 17(1) AI Act).

**CSF anchors (from `SR-AIACT-008` + `SR-AIACT-009` + `SR-AIACT-010` + `SR-AIACT-017` v0.3 security_rationale):**
ID.AM-08 (primary), PR.DS-12, GV.PO-01.
*(ID.AM-08 — Systems, hardware, software, services, and data are managed throughout their life cycle — anchors the AI Act Art. 11 + Art. 19(1) `logs referred to in Article 12(1)` + Art. 17(1) QMS documentation of 13 enumerated aspects mapped to NIST CSF 2.0 ID.AM-08 per CRDA DomainAnalysis §D-09.4 line 113 cross-validation; PR.DS-12 — Data managed consistent with risk strategy — anchors the AI Act Art. 17(1)(d) `data quality` + Art. 17(1)(g) `risk-management-system documentation cross-reference to Art. 9` per CRDA DomainAnalysis §D-09.4 line 113; GV.PO-01 — Organizational cybersecurity policy established, communicated, enforced — anchors the AI Act Art. 17(1) QMS `systematic and orderly manner in the form of written policies, procedures and instructions` mapped to NIST CSF 2.0 GV.PO-01 (cross to D-09.1 AI Act sub-SO). The three subcategories together enable the provider to demonstrate ex post, against the MSA + AI Office supervisory baseline, that the Annex IV technical documentation (Art. 11) + Art. 12(2) traceability + Art. 17(1) 13-element QMS documentation + Art. 19(1) provider logs were established before placing on the market and kept up-to-date during the AI lifecycle. **IMPORTANT — AI Act Recital 78 GDPR preservation**: AI Act Recital 78 explicitly preserves GDPR for personal-data processing — `protection of natural persons in relation to the processing of personal data … is governed by Regulation (EU) 2016/679`; providers that are also controllers maintain **parallel artefacts** (GDPR RoPA + AI Act Annex IV + AI Act Art. 17 QMS). **IMPORTANT — CRA Art. 13(4) sentence 2 risk-assessment consolidation does NOT extend to documentation**: the CRA Art. 13(4) sentence 2 unification rule at D-09.2 covers the *risk-assessment object*; there is no equivalent documentation-level consolidation rule at D-09.4; the two regimes maintain **parallel artefacts** (CRA Annex VII + AI Act Annex IV + AI Act Art. 17 QMS). The **T5 mapping error on AIA-C11 (Art. 12(4) does not exist in the OJ) is surfaced in SR-AIACT-009 `ambiguity_notes` without silent reinterpretation** — the AI Act Art. 12(4) reference is a mapping-error trap; the OJ only has Art. 12(1)/(2)/(3) (per SR-AIACT-009 ambiguity_notes).)*

cross_reg_analysis.raw_md:
## 1. Cross-Regulation Analysis

> **Domain analysis:** [D-09.4 Records of Processing](../../DomainAnalysis/D-09_Governance-Documentation/D-09.4.md)

### D-09.4 Records of Processing

<!-- participants: GDPR, NIS2, CRA, DORA, AI_Act -->

**Participants (from CRDA):** GDPR, NIS2, CRA, DORA, AI_Act

**Sole-authority note (CRDA flag):** DORA is fully absent — no RoPA-equivalent. DORA BC/DR logging (Art. 12) is operational continuity, not processing-of-personal-data records. For DORA financial entities, the GDPR RoPA remains the dedicated authority for processing-activities documentation. The DORA Art. 12 logging obligation is a separate artefact.

#### Pair: GDPR ↔ NIS2

**GDPR article (verbatim, Art. 30(1)–(4) + Art. 12-14):**
> "Each controller … shall maintain a record of processing activities under its responsibility … Such record shall contain … where possible, the envisaged time limits for erasure of the different categories of data …" — Art. 30(1) GDPR
> "Each processor … shall maintain a record of all categories of processing activities carried out on behalf of a controller …" — Art. 30(2) GDPR
> "The records referred to in paragraphs 1 and 2 shall be maintained in writing, including in electronic form …" — Art. 30(3) GDPR

**NIS2 article (verbatim, Art. 24(1)/(2)/(3)):**
> "Member States shall encourage the use of European or internationally accepted standards and specifications relevant to the security of network and information systems …" — Art. 24(1) NIS 2 (per SR-NIS2-043)

**Scope comparison:**
- GDPR scope: controller/processor RoPA mandate with 250-employee exception.
- NIS 2 scope: certification-driven recordkeeping (Art. 24) — not a RoPA-equivalent.
- Scope overlap: **N (typically)** — different record types.

**Trigger comparison:**
- GDPR trigger: processing of personal data.
- NIS 2 trigger: certification requirement (where Member State or Commission delegated act so requires).
- Trigger alignment: **DIFFERENT**.

**Threshold/timeline comparison:**
- GDPR: 250-employee carve-out; retention until processing ceases + on SA request.
- NIS 2: per Member State.
- Tension: **NONE**.

**Recipient comparison:**
- GDPR: data subject, DPA.
- NIS 2: management body, competent authority.

**Content template comparison:**
- GDPR: Art. 30(1)/(2) content items.
- NIS 2: not codified (certification-driven).

**Classified relationship (from CRDA):** DIFFERENT-PERSPECTIVE
**Verified relationship (after OJ-text analysis):** **SAME — DIFFERENT-PERSPECTIVE**
**Reasoning:** GDPR RoPA is processing-activities-focussed; NIS 2 Art. 24 is certification-driven. Different record types; no substantive overlap.

**Scope-disjoint test:** Does this pair actually overlap on a single event at a single party? **N (typically)** — different record types, different triggers. Substantively scope-disjoint.

**Downstream implication (Phase 1C Doc 07):** Phase 2 obligation derivation must produce two separate artefacts: GDPR Art. 30 RoPA + NIS 2 Art. 24 certification-driven records. No OJ-level consolidation.

---

#### Pair: GDPR ↔ CRA

**GDPR article (verbatim, Art. 30(1)–(4)):**
> [as quoted above]

**CRA article (verbatim, Art. 13(12) + Art. 13(13) + Annex VII §1-§8):**
> "Where, prior to placing a product with digital elements on the market, the manufacturer has carried out a conformity assessment procedure … the manufacturer shall draw up the technical documentation … and draw up the EU declaration of conformity." — Art. 13(12) CRA (per SR-CRA-085)
> "The manufacturer shall keep the technical documentation and the EU declaration of conformity at the disposal of the market surveillance authorities for at least 10 years after the product has been placed on the market, or for the support period, whichever is longer." — Art. 13(13) CRA (per SR-CRA-084)

**Scope comparison:**
- GDPR scope: controller/processor RoPA.
- CRA scope: product-level technical documentation + EU declaration + CE marking + Annex VII 8-item content.
- Scope overlap: **N (typically)** — different record types.

**Trigger comparison:**
- GDPR trigger: processing of personal data.
- CRA trigger: product placement on market.
- Trigger alignment: **DIFFERENT**.

**Threshold/timeline comparison:**
- GDPR: 250-employee carve-out; retention until processing ceases + on SA request.
- CRA: 10 years or support period, whichever longer.
- Tension: **NONE**.

**Recipient comparison:**
- GDPR: data subject, DPA.
- CRA: MSA.

**Content template comparison:**
- GDPR: Art. 30(1)/(2) content items.
- CRA: Annex VII 8-item content list.

**Classified relationship (from CRDA):** DIFFERENT-PERSPECTIVE
**Verified relationship (after OJ-text analysis):** **SAME — DIFFERENT-PERSPECTIVE**
**Reasoning:** GDPR RoPA covers processing activities (purposes, data categories, recipients, safeguards); CRA technical documentation covers product cybersecurity (design, development, risk assessment, SBOM, conformity). Different artefacts serving different audit purposes. The two regimes do not cross-substitute.

**Scope-disjoint test:** Does this pair actually overlap on a single event at a single party? **N (typically)** — different record types, different triggers. Substantively scope-disjoint.

**Downstream implication (Phase 1C Doc 07):** Phase 2 obligation derivation must produce two separate artefacts: GDPR Art. 30 RoPA + CRA Annex VII technical documentation. No OJ-level consolidation.

---

#### Pair: GDPR ↔ DORA

> **Note:** CRDA did not explicitly analyze this pair for D-09.4.

**GDPR article (verbatim, Art. 30(1)–(4)):**
> [as quoted above]

**DORA article (verbatim, Art. 9(4)(a) per SR-DORA-031):**
> "Financial entities shall … develop and document an information security policy defining rules to protect the availability, authenticity, integrity and confidentiality of data, information assets and ICT assets, including those of their customers, where applicable." — Art. 9(4)(a) DORA

**Scope comparison:**
- GDPR scope: controller/processor RoPA.
- DORA scope: information security policy defining CIA+A rules (no RoPA-equivalent).
- Scope overlap: **N (typically)** — different record types (RoPA vs. IS-policy documentation).

**Trigger comparison:**
- GDPR trigger: processing of personal data.
- DORA trigger: information security policy adoption.

The records-of-processing architecture is **legitimately diverse with 5 distinct documentation regimes + DORA fully absent for RoPA + no OJ-level cross-regulation consolidation rule**. The 5 regulators impose documentation regimes from **5 distinct regulatory perspectives** under **5 distinct triggers**, at **5 distinct retention cadences**, and serving **5 distinct audit purposes**: **GDPR RoPA on processing activities** (per-controller / per-processor RoPA mandating Art. 30(1) content items; Art. 30(5) 250-employee exception; Art. 30(3) `in writing, including in electronic form`; Art. 12-14 transparency to data subjects; DPA audit; Art. 30(4) on-supervisory-authority-request); **NIS 2 Art. 24 certification-driven records** (per-Member-State certification requirements per Art. 24(1)/(2)/(3); not codified at OJ level for content — entity-discretionary under Member State transposition); **CRA Annex VII 8-item technical documentation** (per-product technical documentation with Annex VII §1 `general description` + §2 `design, development, production, and vulnerability-handling processes` + §3 `cybersecurity risk assessment` + §4 `support-period determination` + §5 `harmonised standards applied` + §6 `reports of tests carried out` + §7 `copy of the EU declaration of conformity` + §8 `SBOM on MSA reasoned request`; MSA audit; 10-year retention or support period (whichever longer) per Art. 13(13)); **DORA Art. 9(4)(a) information security policy** (per-entity IS policy defining rules to protect CIA+A of data/information assets/ICT assets; NCA audit; no OJ-quantified retention period); **AI Act Annex IV + Art. 17(1) QMS + Art. 19(1) logs** (per-AI-system technical documentation + 13-element QMS documentation + high-risk-AI-system logs; MSA + AI Office audit; no OJ-quantified retention period for documentation). **No OJ-level cross-regulation consolidation rule for documentation at D-09.4** — the CRA Art. 13(4) sentence 2 risk-assessment consolidation rule at D-09.2 does NOT extend to the documentation object (per CRDA-deep §D-09.4 line 392: "no OJ-level consolidation rule for documentation — unlike the CRA Art. 13(4) sentence 2 risk-assessment consolidation"). The 5 documentation regimes operate in parallel with distinct record types, distinct triggers, distinct retention cadences, and distinct recipients; **no OJ-permitted cross-substitution exists** between any pair. The **DORA-absence flag** is structurally significant: per CRDA-deep §D-09.4 line 29, DORA is fully absent for RoPA (no DORA Art. 30 analogue); the DORA Art. 9(4)(a) information security policy is a **different record type** serving a different audit purpose than RoPA. For DORA financial entities, the GDPR RoPA remains the dedicated authority for processing-activities documentation; the DORA Art. 9(4)(a) IS-policy documentation is a separate artefact.

### Cross-recipient map

D-09.4 generates a **layered recipient set on the parallel-documentation-regimes evidence trail** but does **not generate a notification cascade of D-09.4 itself**. The recipient set is at the **parallel-documentation-evidence layer**: **GDPR's supervisory authority (DPA)** on the Art. 30(1) RoPA content items + Art. 30(4) on-supervisory-authority-request + Art. 30(5) 250-employee exception + Art. 12-14 transparency to data subjects; **NIS 2's management body + competent authority** on the Art. 24(1)/(2)/(3) certification-driven records (per-Member-State transposition); **CRA's market surveillance authority (MSA)** on the Annex VII §1-§8 8-item content list + Art. 13(12) pre-market documentation + Art. 13(13) `10-year technical-documentation retention` or support period (whichever longer) + Art. 13(15)/(16) product identification + manufacturer contact info + Art. 13(19) support-period end-date display + Art. 13(20) EU declaration provision + Art. 13(21) immediate corrective measures + Art. 13(22) MSA cooperation + Art. 13(23) cessation-of-operations notification + Art. 13(24)/(25) Commission implementing/delegated acts + Art. 28 EU declaration + Art. 30 CE marking + Art. 31 technical-documentation drawing-up-before-placing + Art. 32 conformity-assessment procedure selection; **DORA's competent authority (NCA — national financial supervisory authority)** on the Art. 9(4)(a) `develop and document an information security policy defining rules to protect the availability, authenticity, integrity and confidentiality of data, information assets and ICT assets, including those of their customers, where applicable`; **AI Act's market surveillance authority + AI Office + provider** on the Art. 11 Annex IV technical documentation (per Art. 12(2) traceability) + Art. 17(1) 13-element QMS documentation + Art. 19(1) `Providers of high-risk AI systems shall keep the logs referred to in Article 12(1) … to the extent that such logs are under their control`. These are **distinct regulatory channels on the parallel-documentation-regimes artefacts** but they do not generate a notification cascade of D-09.4 itself — they are **compliance-evidence channels on the layered documentation architecture**. The **D-09.1 (Information Security Policies)** is the upstream policy artefact where the documentation regimes are governed by the documented policies; **D-09.3 (Asset Inventories)** is the inventory-evidence downstream artefact where the CRA Annex VII §1-§2 architecture documentation is a sub-register and the DORA Art. 7(1) ICT asset register documentation is the entity-level master record.

**Objective.** Records of processing activities are established through **parallel documentation regimes** that produce records of processing activities, technical documentation, certification records, information security policy documentation, and quality management system documentation serving distinct audit purposes.

**Considerations.**
- **5 of 5 regulations participate** on **parallel artefacts** with distinct triggers, retention cadences, and recipients — GDPR Art. 30(1)/(2)/(3)/(4)/(5) RoPA (DPA audit); NIS 2 Art. 24(1)/(2)/(3) certification-driven records per Member State (Member State / competent authority audit); CRA Annex VII §1-§8 8-item technical documentation + Art. 13(12)/(13)/(15)-(25)/(28)/(30)/(31)/(32) (MSA audit, 10-year or support-period retention); DORA Art. 9(4)(a) information security policy (NCA audit — **different record type from RoPA, DORA is fully absent for RoPA-equivalent per CRDA explicit note**); AI Act Art. 11 + Art. 12(1)/(2) + Art. 17(1) 13-element QMS + Art. 19(1) provider logs (MSA + AI Office audit).

For DORA financial entities, the GDPR RoPA remains the dedicated authority for processing-activities documentation; DORA Art. 9(4)(a) IS-policy documentation is a separate artefact.
- **No OJ-level cross-regulation consolidation rule for documentation** — unlike D-09.2 where CRA Art. 13(4) sentence 2 permits AI Act Art. 9 risk-management to substitute for CRA risk assessment, that rule covers the *risk-assessment object* not the *documentation object*. The 5 documentation regimes operate in parallel; no OJ-permitted cross-substitution exists.
- **Scope caveats**: (a) 3 substantively scope-disjoint pairs (GDPR ↔ NIS 2 + GDPR ↔ CRA + GDPR ↔ DORA) — different record types, different audit purposes; the high-level SO does NOT engage on these pairs alone; (b) the only cross-regulation link involving CRA + NIS 2 is the CRA Art. 27(8)/(9) CSA-certification presumption-of-conformity as upstream gate for NSIE procurements; (c) the 5 documentation regimes must be maintained in parallel even when the product is CRA-conformant and the AI system is AI-Act high-risk.

### Scope-overlap matrix (5×5)

(See the validated-relationships table above for the 5×5 matrix.)

### Scope-overlap principle (operational). The high-level SO
(`SO-D-09.4.HL`) engages on a given party **if and only if ≥2 applicable
regulations have ≥1 Y or Conditional-Y-scope-overlap pair on the same
party** where both regimens operate on the **same documentation object
serving the same audit purpose**. For an integrated AI-enabled CRA-
regulated product that is also a high-risk AI system (an AI provider
that is also a manufacturer + high-risk AI provider), the **CRA ↔ AI
Act Y pair** engages the high-level SO with the documentation stack
(CRA Annex VII + AI Act Annex IV + AI Act Art. 17 QMS — three parallel
artefacts serving distinct audit purposes: CRA MSA audit + AI Act MSA +
AI Office audit). For a DORA financial entity that is also an NSIE and
that procures CRA-conformant products (DORA financial entity + NSIE +
CRA-conformant-product procurements), the **NIS 2 ↔ DORA Conditional-Y
+ CRA ↔ DORA Conditional-Y** pairs engage the high-level SO with the
parallel documentation regimes (NIS 2 Art. 24 certification-driven
records + DORA Art. 9(4)(a) IS-policy + CRA Annex VII 8-item technical
documentation). For a controller that is also an NSIE and deploys
high-risk AI (a controller + NSIE + high-risk-AI deployer), the **GDPR
↔ AI Act Conditional-Y + NIS 2 ↔ AI Act Conditional-Y** pairs engage
the high-level SO with the parallel documentation regimes (GDPR RoPA +
AI Act Annex IV + AI Act Art. 17 QMS + NIS 2 Art. 24 certification
records). For a DORA financial entity that is also a controller
deploying high-risk AI (a DORA financial entity + controller + high-
risk-AI deployer/processor), the **DORA ↔ AI Act Conditional-Y + GDPR
↔ DORA N (typically)-recommended** pairs engage the high-level SO
with **specific scope** — GDPR RoPA as the dedicated authority for
processing-activities documentation (per CRDA explicit DORA-absence
flag) + DORA Art. 9(4)(a) IS-policy documentation as a separate
artefact + AI Act Annex IV + AI Act Art. 17 QMS. **The 3 substantively
scope-disjoint pairs (GDPR ↔ NIS 2 + GDPR ↔ CRA + GDPR ↔ DORA) never
engage the high-level SO together** — different record types serving
different audit purposes; CRDA `different-perspective` is a taxonomy
artifact (per CRDA-deep §D-09.4 lines 68, 110, 153) for these three
pairs. The 5 documentation regimes are stacked in parallel, not
collapsed: GDPR Art. 30 RoPA (controller / processor RoPA); NIS 2
Art. 24 certification-driven records (per Member State); CRA Annex VII
8-item technical documentation + EU declaration + CE marking (per
manufacturer); DORA Art. 9(4)(a) IS-policy documentation (per financial
entity); AI Act Annex IV + Art. 17(1) QMS + Art. 19(1) logs (per
provider). The high-level SO surfaces the **common documentation-regime
duty** but does **NOT** collapse the 5 documentation regimes into a
single artefact; the per-reg perspective on the artefact, anchor,
governance body, content template, retention cadence, recipient, and
evidence template is preserved verbatim.

### Aggregation logic (from CRDA-deep verified classifications)

- **5 of 5 regulations participate** (GDPR, NIS 2, CRA, DORA, AI Act —
  all five participate on the documentation object): GDPR (Art. 30(1)
  + Art. 30(2) + Art. 30(3) + Art. 30(4) + Art. 30(5) + Art. 12-14 as
  controller/processor RoPA mandating the 7-item content); NIS 2 (Art.
  24(1)/(2)/(3) as per-Member-State certification-driven recordkeeping
  — not a RoPA-equivalent); CRA (Annex VII §1-§8 8-item technical
  documentation + Art. 13(12) + Art. 13(13) 10-year retention +
  Art. 13(15)/(16) + Art. 13(17) + Art. 13(19) + Art. 13(20) +
  Art. 13(21) + Art. 13(22) + Art. 13(23) + Art. 13(24)/(25) +
  Art. 28 + Art. 30 + Art. 31 + Art. 32 as product-level technical
  documentation regime); DORA (Art. 9(4)(a) as entity-level
  information security policy — different record type, **fully absent
  for RoPA-equivalent** per CRDA explicit note); AI Act (Art. 11 +
  Art. 12(1)/(2) + Art. 17(1) + Art. 19(1) as AI-system-specific
  technical documentation + 13-element QMS + provider logs).
- **10 pairwise cells, verified by CRDA-deep**:
  GDPR↔NIS2 (SAME — DIFFERENT-PERSPECTIVE — substantively scope-
  disjoint per CRDA `N (typically)` — different record types, RoPA vs.
  certification-driven Art. 24; substantively scope-disjoint);
  GDPR↔CRA (SAME — DIFFERENT-PERSPECTIVE — substantively scope-
  disjoint per CRDA `N (typically)` — different record types, RoPA vs.
  CRA Annex VII 8-item technical documentation; substantively scope-
  disjoint);
  GDPR↔DORA (SAME — DIFFERENT-PERSPECTIVE RECOMMENDED-by-CRDA —
  substantively scope-disjoint per CRDA `N (typically)`; RoPA vs.
  DORA Art. 9(4)(a) IS-policy documentation; DORA fully absent for

  GDPR↔AI_Act (SAME — COMPLEMENTARY — Conditional-Y when
  provider/deployer is controller processing personal data via high-
  risk AI; GDPR RoPA + AI Act Annex IV + AI Act Art. 17 QMS stack
  on three parallel artefacts; AI Act Recital 78 explicit GDPR

  NIS2↔CRA (SAME — COMPLEMENTARY — Conditional-Y when NSIE procures

  CSA-certification presumption-of-conformity machinery as upstream

  NIS2↔DORA (SAME — COMPLEMENTARY RECOMMENDED-by-CRDA — Conditional-Y
  when DORA financial entity is also an NSIE);
  NIS2↔AI_Act (SAME — COMPLEMENTARY RECOMMENDED-by-CRDA —
  Conditional-Y when NSIE deploys high-risk AI);
  CRA↔DORA (SAME — COMPLEMENTARY RECOMMENDED-by-CRDA — Conditional-Y
  when DORA entity procures CRA-conformant products; CRA Annex VII

  CRA↔AI_Act (SAME — COMPLEMENTARY — Y when CRA-regulated product is
  also a high-risk AI system; CRA Annex VII + AI Act Annex IV + AI

  DORA↔AI_Act (SAME — COMPLEMENTARY RECOMMENDED-by-CRDA —
  Conditional-Y when DORA financial entity deploys high-risk AI; DORA

- The **DORA-absence flag** is structurally significant: per CRDA-
  deep §D-09.4 line 29, **DORA is fully absent for RoPA-equivalent**
  — DORA does not impose a RoPA obligation (no analogue to GDPR Art.

  risk-assessment consolidation"). The 5 documentation regimes

- The 5 participating regulators impose **distinct documentation

  bodies**, **distinct content templates**, **distinct retention
  cadences**, and **distinct recipients**: GDPR (Art. 30(1) 7-item

  writing, including in electronic form`; DPA audit;
  substantively scope-disjoint from NIS 2 / CRA / DORA-recommended /

### Emergent tensions (from CRDA-deep §D-09.4)

**0 conditional emergent tensions** on the documentation duty itself — no pair was verified as CONTRADICTORY or CORRECTED. The 10 pairs are all SAME — 2 SAME — DIFFERENT-PERSPECTIVE + 3 SAME — COMPLEMENTARY-classified-by-CRDA + 1 SAME — DIFFERENT-PERSPECTIVE-recommended-by-CRDA + 4 SAME — COMPLEMENTARY-recommended-by-CRDA. **No CORRECTED, no CONTRADICTORY** pairs. **No scope-disjoint CONTRADICTORY** — the 3 `N (typically)` pairs (GDPR ↔ NIS 2 + GDPR ↔ CRA + GDPR ↔ DORA-recommended) are substantively scope-disjoint on the documentation-object dimension, not on the policy-object dimension; the high-level SO does **NOT** engage on these pairs because no same-event overlap exists, but this is **not** an emergent tension requiring Phase 1C resolution.

The **DORA-absence flag (CRDA-deep §D-09.4 line 29)** is the structurally significant operational divergence — DORA is fully absent for RoPA-equivalent (no analogue to GDPR Art. 30); the DORA Art. 9(4)(a) information security policy is a **different record type** serving a **different audit purpose**. For DORA financial entities, the GDPR RoPA remains the dedicated authority for processing-activities documentation; the DORA Art. 9(4)(a) IS-policy documentation is a separate artefact (cross to D-09.1 DORA sub-SO). Phase 1 entities with GDPR + DORA applicability must maintain both GDPR RoPA + DORA Art. 9(4)(a) IS-policy documentation on distinct artefacts that are not substitutable.

The **no OJ-level consolidation rule for documentation flag (CRDA-deep §D-09.4 line 392)** is the structurally significant operational divergence — unlike D-09.2 where CRA Art. 13(4) sentence 2 permits AI Act Art. 9 risk-management to substitute for CRA risk assessment (the only OJ-level cross-regulation consolidation rule in D-09.2), the documentation object at D-09.4 has **no OJ-level cross-regulation consolidation rule**. Phase 1 entities with ≥2 of the 5 participating regs applicability must maintain **parallel documentation regimes** even when the underlying product is CRA-conformant and the underlying AI system is AI-Act high-risk. **No cross-substitution is OJ-permitted**.

The **3 substantively scope-disjoint pairs (GDPR ↔ NIS 2 + GDPR ↔ CRA + GDPR ↔ DORA-recommended)** are the structurally significant operational divergence — these 3 pairs operate on **different record types** (RoPA vs. certification-driven records + CRA Annex VII 8-item technical documentation + DORA Art. 9(4)(a) IS-policy documentation) serving **different audit purposes** (DPA audit on processing activities vs. Member-State-driven certification audit + MSA audit on product cybersecurity + NCA audit on IS policy). CRDA `different-perspective` is a taxonomy artifact (per CRDA-deep §D-09.4 lines 68, 110, 153) for these three pairs. Phase 1 entities with GDPR + CRA + NIS 2 applicability must maintain **three separate documentation regimes** (GDPR RoPA + CRA Annex VII technical documentation + NIS 2 Art. 24 certification-driven records on distinct artefacts serving distinct audit purposes).

The **5 parallel documentation regimes** are the structurally significant compliance-architecture: **GDPR Art. 30 RoPA on processing activities** + **NIS 2 Art. 24 certification-driven records** + **CRA Annex VII 8-item technical documentation + EU declaration + CE marking** + **DORA Art. 9(4)(a) IS-policy documentation** + **AI Act Annex IV + Art. 17(1) QMS + Art. 19(1) logs**. The 5 regimes operate in parallel with distinct record types, distinct triggers, distinct retention cadences, and distinct recipients. **No OJ-level cross-substitution exists** between any pair. Phase 1 entities with all 5 participating regs applicability (a DORA financial entity that is also a controller + NSIE + manufacturer + high-risk-AI provider) must maintain **5 parallel documentation regimes** serving 5 distinct audit purposes.

The **CRA Art. 27(8)/(9) CSA-certification presumption-of-conformity machinery** is the **only cross-regulation link at D-09.4 involving CRA + NIS 2** — when an NSIE procures CSA-certified CRA products, the CRA Art. 27(8)/(9) `A product with digital elements and the processes put in place by the manufacturer for which the manufacturer has obtained an EU statement of conformity or an EU certificate under a European cybersecurity certification scheme adopted pursuant to Regulation (EU) 2019/881, at assurance level at least 'substantial', shall be presumed to be in conformity with the essential cybersecurity requirements` is the upstream gate for NIS 2 procurements.

The high-level SO surfaces the **common documentation-regime duty** (records of processing activities + technical documentation + certification records + IS-policy documentation + QMS documentation + provider logs) on **parallel artefacts** serving distinct audit purposes; the per-reg perspective on the artefact, anchor, governance body, content template, retention cadence, recipient, and evidence template is documented separately in the 5 sub-SOs and the per-recipient evidence packs. **0 conditional emergent tensions** because no pair was verified as CONTRADICTORY or CORRECTED — the 5-distinct-documentation-regimes structure (GDPR Art. 30 RoPA + NIS 2 Art. 24 + CRA Annex VII 8-item + DORA Art. 9(4)(a) + AI Act Annex IV + Art. 17(1) QMS + Art. 19(1) logs) with **DORA-absence flag + no OJ-level consolidation rule for documentation + 3 substantively scope-disjoint pairs (GDPR ↔ NIS 2 / CRA / DORA-recommended)** is itself the structurally significant compliance-architecture that supersedes any single-regulator reading.

### Downstream implication for Phase 1C Doc 07

A company with ≥2 applicable regulations in scope of D-09.4 — with overlapping documentation scope-determiners on the same party — operates **5 parallel documentation regimes** (GDPR Art. 30 RoPA + NIS 2 Art. 24 + CRA Annex VII 8-item technical documentation + DORA Art. 9(4)(a) IS-policy documentation + AI Act Annex IV + Art. 17(1) QMS + Art. 19(1) logs) on **distinct artefacts** serving **distinct audit purposes**. The high-level SO (`SO-D-09.4.HL`) describes the **common documentation-regime duty** (records of processing activities + technical documentation + certification records + IS-policy documentation + QMS documentation + provider logs) on parallel artefacts; the per-reg perspective is preserved in the 5 sub-SOs (artefact, governance body, anchor, content template, retention cadence, recipient, evidence template). **The 0 conditional emergent tensions are documented as a layered reconciliation**, with the 5-parallel-documentation-regimes operationalising the per-reg governance body on the integrated documentation architecture. The high-level SO does NOT collapse the 5 documentation regimes into a single artefact; the per-reg perspective on the artefact, governance body, anchor, content template, retention cadence, recipient, and evidence template is preserved verbatim. Phase 1C Doc 07 documents the **documentation-architecture strategy**: (1) a **5-parallel-documentation-regimes architecture** with explicit governance-body hand-offs (GDPR Art. 30(1) RoPA + Art. 12-14 transparency → NIS 2 Art. 24(1)/(2)/(3) certification-driven records → CRA Annex VII §1-§8 8-item technical documentation + Art. 13(12)/(13)/(15)/(16)/(17)/(19)/(20)/(21)/(22)/(23)/EU declaration/CE marking → DORA Art. 9(4)(a) IS-policy documentation → AI Act Annex IV + Art. 17(1) QMS + Art. 19(1) logs); (2) the **DORA-absence flag** preserving GDPR RoPA as the dedicated authority for processing-activities documentation even for DORA financial entities (GDPR Art. 30 RoPA + DORA Art. 9(4)(a) IS-policy documentation on parallel artefacts serving distinct audit purposes); (3) the **no OJ-level consolidation rule for documentation** distinguishing D-09.4 from D-09.2 (the CRA Art. 13(4) sentence 2 risk-assessment consolidation rule covers the *risk-assessment object* but not the *documentation object* at D-09.4); (4) the **3 substantively scope-disjoint pairs** (GDPR ↔ NIS 2 + GDPR ↔ CRA + GDPR ↔ DORA-recommended) preserving GDPR RoPA on processing activities from cross-substitution by NIS 2 Art. 24 + CRA Annex VII + DORA Art. 9(4)(a); (5) the **CRA Art. 27(8)/(9) CSA-certification presumption-of-conformity machinery** as the only cross-regulation link at D-09.4 involving CRA + NIS 2 — when an NSIE procures CSA-certified CRA products, the CRA Art. 27(8)/(9) is the upstream gate for NIS 2 procurements (Conditional-Y scope overlap); (6) the **5 distinct retention cadences** (GDPR until processing ceases + on-DPA-request; CRA 10 years or support period, whichever longer; AI Act no OJ-quantified retention for documentation; DORA no OJ-quantified retention; NIS 2 per Member State) — Phase 1C Doc 07 must perform the Layer 1 cross-cutting retention-cadence reconciliation; (7) the **CRDA NOT-CLASSIFIED pairs** (GDPR ↔ DORA, NIS 2 ↔ DORA, NIS 2 ↔ AI Act, CRA ↔ DORA, DORA ↔ AI Act) — 4 of 5 are recommended-COMPLEMENTARY + 1 is recommended-DIFFERENT-PERSPECTIVE per CRDA explicit Downstream-implication lines; GDPR additionally maps GV.PO-02 for demonstrable-compliance; CRA additionally maps ID.RA-05 for risk-assessment documentation.

The HL does NOT collapse the 5 documentation regimes into a single artefact; the per-reg perspective on the artefact, anchor, governance body, content template, threshold, retention cadence, recipient, and evidence template is preserved verbatim. For a DORA financial entity that is also a controller + NSIE + manufacturer + high-risk-AI provider (all 5 participating regs applicable), the 5 regimes must all be maintained in parallel serving 5 distinct audit purposes.

### 9.4.1 — Controller/processor records of processing activities (RoPA) with the Art. 30(1) 7-item content list, in writing/electronic form, on-supervisory-authority-request

```yaml
- req_id: 9.4.1
  volere_section: 15
  volere_subtype: audit

  title: "Controller and processor maintain a record of processing activities (RoPA) under Art. 30(1)/(2)/(3)/(4)/(5) GDPR with the 7-item content list, on-DPA-request, subject to the 250-employee exception"

  description: |
    The product shall maintain a record of processing activities under the controller's or processor's responsibility (per GDPR Art. 30(1) + Art. 30(2) processor RoPA), in writing or electronic form (per Art. 30(3) `in writing, including in electronic form`), with the Art. 30(1) (a)-(g) 7-item content list (name/contact details of controller; purposes of processing; categories of data subjects and personal data; categories of recipients; transfers to third country; envisaged time limits for erasure; general description of technical and organisational security measures), made available to the supervisory authority on request (per Art. 30(4)), subject to the Art. 30(5) 250-employee exception, and operationalising the Art. 5(2) accountability burden (per Art. 12-14 transparency to data subjects).

  fit_criterion: |
    A RoPA is maintained per Art. 30(1) `record of processing activities under its responsibility` with the 7-item content list (SR-GDPR-048) verified by ID.AM-08 lifecycle-management dimension (controller demonstrates ex post that systems, hardware, software, services, and data are managed throughout their life cycle — the RoPA records the processing activities supporting that life cycle per CRDA DomainAnalysis §9.4 line 113 cross-validation) + PR.DS-12 risk-strategy-consistent data management (the Art. 30(1)(g) `general description of the technical and organisational security measures` is consistent with the entity's risk strategy) + GV.PO-02 process-and-procedure demonstrable-compliance (the Art. 30(3) `in writing, including in electronic form` form-factor is documented and demonstrable to the DPA on request); the processor maintains a parallel record per Art. 30(2) `record of processing activities carried out on behalf of the controller` with the (a)-(e) content (name/contact details of processor + controller; categories of processing; transfers to third country; TOMs; audit enablement to the extent necessary); records are made available to the supervisory authority on request per Art. 30(4) (SR-GDPR-050); the Art. 30(5) 250-employee exception is read at the R1 literal — `an undertaking or organisation employing fewer than 250 persons` is exempt from the RoPA obligation UNLESS processing is likely to result in a risk to the rights and freedoms of data subjects, the processing is not occasional, OR the processing includes special categories of data or criminal-conviction data; the RoPA operationalises Art. 5(2) accountability burden with Art. 12-14 transparency to data subjects cross-referenced (SR-GDPR-065-068); the recipient is the **DPA (supervisory authority)** on the Art. 30(1) RoPA content items + Art. 30(4) on-DPA-request + Art. 30(5) 250-employee exception + Art. 12-14 transparency; verified by DPA audit pack (RoPA extract, on-DPA-request retrieval procedure, accountability burden evidence, Art. 5(2) continuous compliance evidence) + automated RoPA field-completeness test (all 7 Art. 30(1)(a)-(g) fields populated for each processing activity) + Art. 30(3) form-factor inspection (writing or electronic form). **IMPORTANT — DORA-absence flag**: per CRDA-deep §9.4 line 29, DORA is fully absent for RoPA-equivalent — for DORA financial entities, the GDPR RoPA remains the dedicated authority for processing-activities documentation. The GDPR RoPA does NOT substitute for the CRA Annex VII 8-item technical documentation (substantively scope-disjoint per CRDA `N (typically)`), the NIS 2 Art. 24 certification-driven records (substantively scope-disjoint per CRDA `N (typically)`), the DORA Art. 9(4)(a) IS-policy documentation (different record type, different audit purpose), or the AI Act Annex IV + Art. 17(1) QMS documentation (parallel artefacts; no OJ-level consolidation rule for documentation at 9.4).

  satisfaction:
    controls: []
    nodes: []

  considerations: |
    The GDPR records-of-processing obligation at 9.4 is the **controller/processor RoPA** with **DPO as governance anchor** under Art. 30(1) + Art. 30(2) + Art. 30(3) + Art. 30(4) + Art. 30(5) + Art. 5(2) accountability + Art. 12-14 transparency to data subjects. Art. 30(1) `record of processing activities under its responsibility` (per SR-GDPR-048); Art. 30(1) (a)-(g) 7-item content list is the formal codification — `(a) name and contact details of the controller, any joint controller, the controller's representative and the data protection officer; (b) purposes of the processing; (c) a description of the categories of data subjects and of the categories of personal data; (d) the categories of recipients to whom personal data have been or will be disclosed including recipients in third countries or international organisations; (e) transfers of personal data to a third country or an international organisation …; (f) where possible, the envisaged time limits for erasure of the different categories of data; (g) where possible, a general description of the technical and organisational security measures`. Art. 30(2) processor RoPA content — `the name and contact details of the processor or processors, of each controller on behalf of which the processor is acting, the categories of processing carried out on behalf of the controller, transfers to a third country … and the technical and organisational security measures`. Art. 30(3) `in writing, including in electronic form` — entity-discretionary on form factor. Art. 30(4) on-DPA-request — the records are made available to the supervisory authority on request. Art. 30(5) 250-employee exception — `an undertaking or organisation employing fewer than 250 persons is not obliged to keep records of processing activities, unless … (a) the processing is likely to result in a risk to the rights and freedoms of data subjects; (b) the processing is not occasional; or (c) the processing includes special categories of data as referred to in Article 9(1) or personal data relating to criminal convictions and offences`. Art. 12-14 transparency to data subjects — the RoPA is upstream of the data-subject-facing privacy notice (cross to 9.1 GDPR sub-SO). **DORA-absence flag** (per CRDA-deep §9.4 line 29): for DORA financial entities, the GDPR RoPA remains the dedicated authority for processing-activities documentation; the DORA Art. 9(4)(a) IS-policy documentation is a separate artefact. The recipient is the **DPA (supervisory authority)** on the Art. 30(1) RoPA content items + Art. 30(4) on-DPA-request + Art. 30(5) 250-employee exception + Art. 12-14 transparency.
```

  applicable_if:
    regs: [CRA]

    The CRA records-of-processing obligation at 9.4 is the **product-level technical documentation** per Annex VII §1-§8 8-item content list (fully content-codified), with 10-year or support-period retention (whichever longer) per Art. 13(13), drawn up before placing on the market per Art. 31(2) + Art. 13(12) sentence 1, and the manufacturer as governance body per Art. 13. Art. 13(12) sentence 2 requires the manufacturer to carry out the conformity assessment procedure per Art. 32 + draw up the EU declaration of conformity per Art. 28 + affix the CE marking per Art. 30. Art. 13(13) `10 years … or for the support period, whichever is longer` — the retention period is the maximum of 10 years or the support period. Art. 13(19) + Art. 13(20) + Art. 13(21) + Art. 13(22) + Art. 13(23) cover support-period end-date display + EU declaration provision + immediate corrective measures + MSA cooperation + cessation-of-operations notification. Art. 13(24) + Art. 13(25) + Art. 14(9) + Art. 14(10) + Art. 7(3) + Art. 7(4) + Art. 30(6) cover Commission implementing/delegated acts. Art. 28 + Art. 30 + Art. 31 + Art. 32 cover EU declaration of conformity + CE marking + technical-documentation drawing-up-before-placing + conformity-assessment procedure selection. **CRA + AI Act for AI-enabled products do NOT have OJ-level consolidation for documentation**: the CRA Art. 13(4) sentence 2 unification rule at 9.2 covers the *risk-assessment object*; there is no equivalent documentation-level consolidation rule at 9.4; the two regimes maintain parallel artefacts (CRA Annex VII + AI Act Annex IV + AI Act Art. 17(1) QMS). The recipient is the **MSA (market surveillance authority)** on the Annex VII §1-§8 8-item content list + Art. 13(12)-(25) + Art. 28 EU declaration + Art. 30 CE marking.

## Decisions (CORR-029)

### Orphan SO justifications

**DORA**: SO-D-09.4.DORA inherits from (out-of-scope as RoPA-equivalent — partial via DORA Art. 9(4)(a) SO-DORA-018 cross). DORA does not impose a Records-of-Processing-Activities (RoPA)-equivalent obligation analogous to GDPR Art. 30. DORA's information-security-policy obligation (Art. 9(4)(a)) is operationalised through SO-DORA-018 (lives in D-09.1 Policies). D-09.4 is therefore a DORA-orphan at the surface level but the DORA-side documentation discipline is captured under the policy layer (D-09.1). No standalone DORA SO for D-09.4 is needed because the OJ text does not impose RoPA-equivalent records on financial entities.

**AI_Act**: SO-D-09.4.AI_Act inherits from SO-AIACT-009 + SO-AIACT-010 + SO-AIACT-008 (logs). The AI_Act technical-documentation obligation in D-09.4 is preserved as a multi-inheritance from the three primary technical-documentation and log-keeping SOs in D-09.3 / D-09.4 / D-10.2. The AI Act Annex IV technical-documentation package overlaps with the D-09.3 / D-09.4 / D-10.2 sub-domains; the AI_Act side is surfaced as multi-inheritance rather than a standalone SO. The 3-inheritance pattern reflects the AI Act's vertical-stack nature (upstream training, runtime governance, post-market monitoring).
